etymology.description: 星(星) (xīng, "star") + 期(期) (qī, "period") + 天(天) (tiān, "day; sky") forms a term used for naming days of the week in modern Chinese. 星(星) (xīng, "star") originally refers to the stars in the sky and implies celestial patterns, while 期(期) (qī, "period") denotes a recurring interval or cycle. 天(天) (tiān, "day; sky") signifies a day within that cycle.
etymology.interpretation: 星(星) (xīng, "star") hints at a heavenly cycle like the repeating patterns of stars, combined with 期(期) (qī, "period") signifying a set timeframe. The phrase thus describes a cyclical series of days. 天(天) (tiān, "day") specifies the particular day in this sequence, here meaning 'a day within the cosmic week,' specifically 星期天, the day we call Sunday.

examples.chinese: 星期天(星期天)我们去公园(我們去公園)。
examples.english: On Sunday, we go to the park.

examples.chinese: 这个星期天(這個星期天)会很热(會很熱)。
examples.english: This Sunday will be very hot.

examples.chinese: 星期天(星期天)是我最喜欢的日子(是我最喜歡的日子)。
examples.pinyin: Xīngqītiān shì wǒ zuì xǐhuān de rìzi.
